title: Chapter 12: Until Dawn
content: The text arrives three days after the gala, while I'm standing in a vintage shop on Ludlow Street, running my fingers along a 1940s beaded clutch that reminds me of someone I used to be.
**Come over. I'll cook. Don't argue.**
I argue.

I argue for eleven minutes. I send him a series of increasingly porous objections — I'm busy, I don't eat carbohydrates, his building probably has a doorman who will judge my shoes — and he responds to each one with a single word until I run out of excuses and he sends the address and I'm already hailing a cab.

The penthouse is on the 42nd floor. The elevator opens directly into his living space and I step out into air that smells like nothing — clean and expensive, temperature-controlled, the ambient scent of money that doesn't have to try. Floor-to-ceiling windows wrap the far wall and Manhattan glitters beneath them like something shattered and unrepentant. Sirens thread up from thirty stories down, thin and distant, the city's constant fever dream rendered almost peaceful by height.
Rowan is in the kitchen.
The kitchen that has clearly never been used.

There is a pot on the stove. There is a wooden spoon in his hand. There is sauce — if you are generous — spattering the pristine countertop in a pattern that suggests either combat or a very specific art movement. He's wearing a cashmere sweater with the sleeves pushed up and there's flour on his jaw and he looks up when I step into the room and something in my chest performs a maneuver it has no business performing.
"You came," he says.
"You texted."
"That's not an answer."
"It's the only one you're getting."

He turns back to the stove. I watch him stir — aggressive, chaotic, like he's punishing the pasta for existing. The hum is there, as it always is around him, a low vibration in the marrow of me, the frequency that says *this one, this one, this one*. I set my bag on the counter. I move closer. The sauce is burning.
"Rowan."
"Don't tell me it's burning."
"It's burning."
"Then fix it."

I reach for the wooden spoon and our fingers brush and the hum stutters — skips like a heartbeat — but it doesn't surge. I can manage this. Maybe. I take the spoon from his hand and reduce the heat and stir with the patience he lacks and he leans against the counter beside me, close enough that I can smell the faint woodiness of his cologne layered over the charred tomato.
"Have you ever actually cooked before?" I ask.
"I've observed cooking."
"That's not the same thing."
"I'm a fast learner."
"You're a terrible cook."
"That's also true."
We eat on the kitchen floor.

Not at the dining table — a black slab of marble that seats twelve and looks like it was designed for hostile board meetings. "A monument to corporate dining," Rowan calls it, and gestures toward the floor with a fork, and I lower myself down across from him because I have eaten on worse floors in worse centuries and the cold marble through my tights is almost comforting, grounding, a reminder that I am here, in a body, in a kitchen that smells like burnt olive oil and the expensive emptiness of a life not yet lived in.
The pasta is mediocre.

The pasta is, if I am honest, not good. The noodles are overcooked and the sauce is both burnt and under-seasoned and there is a structural integrity issue that I choose not to examine closely. But it's warm, and he made it, and I eat it anyway, and I watch him eat it too, and there is something unbearably tender about a man who cannot cook attempting to cook for someone who has eaten at the tables of empires.
"So," he says, and sets his fork down. "Where did you grow up?"
"Everywhere."
"Everywhere."
"Everywhere. My family moved. Constantly."
He nods. He doesn't ask why. Instead: "What's your first memory?"

The question lands somewhere behind my ribs. I consider lying. I consider the dozen false histories I've constructed, the convenient fictions that fit the passport and the paperwork. But this is Rowan, who looked at me across a gala and didn't look away, and the hum is quiet tonight and I am tired of lying to people I want to tell the truth to.
"Firelight," I say.
"Firelight," he repeats. Not a question this time. A tasting. He rolls the word around like he's learning its shape.
"What do you dream about?" he asks.
Something cold moves through me. "You don't want to know."
Rowan leans forward. His eyes are dark and steady and there is no flinching in them, no retreat. "I've been told what I don't want to know my whole life. I'd like to choose for once."
The silence that follows has a weight. I set my plate aside. I hold his gaze.
"Loss," I say. "I dream about loss."

He doesn't ask me to explain. He picks up his fork again and takes another bite of terrible pasta and the moment doesn't close — it opens, widens, makes room for something I haven't felt in a long time: the specific relief of being believed without having to prove it.
Hours pass.

We migrate from the kitchen floor to the living room couch to the window, and the city shivers beneath us and I tell him things. Not everything. Not the architecture of the truth — not the centuries, not the shifts, not the way I have been other people the way water is other shapes. But fragments. True ones, carefully selected, like garments pulled from a trunk and held up to the light.

I tell him about learning to sew. A woman — not my mother — who sat beside me in a room that smelled like lavender and damp wool, who threaded the needle for me when my fingers were too small, who told me that a crooked stitch is just a stitch that decided to go for a walk. I do not tell him the century. I do not tell him that the woman's name was Margery, or that she died of a fever in a winter I have never fully thawed from, or that I still make her pattern adjustments in the margins of my sketches.

I tell him about watching a city burn. The sky gone orange, the heat on my face like a hand pressing me back, the sound of timber giving way like bones. I do not say London. I do not say 1666. I do not say that I stood on the south bank and felt the heat dry my eyes and knew that the world I had built in that body was ending and that I would not follow it into ash.

I tell him about losing someone I loved. About not being able to follow. About the specific violence of staying when the person you — when the person — about grief that doesn't end because you don't, because you keep going and they don't and the world fills up with their absences in the shapes of chairs they won't sit in and words they won't say and mornings that arrive without them like letters addressed to the dead.
I tell him fragments, and Rowan listens.

He listens the way no one in my current life has ever listened. Not with solutions, not with strategies, not with the practiced empathy of people who want to make your pain productive. Rowan doesn't try to fix the unfixable. He doesn't flinch. He doesn't reach for me like I'm something that needs steadying. He sits beside me with his back against the window and his knees drawn up and he listens like silence is a craft he has studied, like the space between my words is a room he is willing to occupy without furniture.
And it undoes me.
Slowly. The way a seam gives — not torn, just released, stitch by stitch, until the shape of the thing changes and more of me is visible than I intended.
"It's late," I say, not because I want it to be over but because I am afraid of how much more I might say.
"It's not," he says. "It's exactly the right time."
He falls asleep at 4 AM.

Mid-sentence. He is telling me something about his mother — about expectations, about inheritances that aren't money, about the particular suffocation of an empire that was built for someone else and handed to you like a gift you can't refuse. "She never understood," he says, and then his voice slows like a record losing speed, and then his head tips back against the window and his breathing shifts into the long, even tide of sleep.

He doesn't finish the sentence. He doesn't need to. I know how it ends. I have heard it before — different voices, different centuries, the same aching incompleteness of a person trying to inhabit a shape that was made for someone else's body.
I don't move.

I sit beside him and I watch him sleep and I know — I *know* — that this is the most intimate thing I have ever done. Not the fragments. Not the almost-confessions. This. Watching. The vulnerability of his unconscious face, the architecture of his rest. The way his lashes rest against his cheeks. The way his hand has settled on his chest, fingers loose, the emerald ring catching the city's light in a slow green pulse like a heart that isn't his.
I have watched this soul sleep before.

In Venice, in a room above a canal in a body that was not this body and a century that has since crumbled into museum exhibits and overpriced gondola rides. In a Gothic manor in the north of England, firelight catching the edge of a face that was his and wasn't, the same breathing, the same surrender to gravity. In a wartime flat in a city that no longer exists on any current map, the sound of bombing outside and the sound of his breathing inside and me caught between the two, trying to memorize him faster than the world could take him away.
Different faces. Same breathing. Same way of giving himself to sleep like it's the only honesty he's permitted.

I memorize him now. The line of his jaw, sharp and unguarded. The flour still faintly visible at his hairline. The way his lips part slightly, as if even in sleep he is trying to say something he hasn't finished. The emerald winking, winking, winking — the color of a promise I can't keep.
This might be the last time I see him in this body.
His or mine.

The thought arrives like a splinter and I don't pull it out. I let it sit. I let it hurt. I let myself feel the full, terrible weight of knowing that whatever this is — this kitchen-floor tenderness, this listening that feels like shelter — it exists on a timer I cannot see.
I write the note on a napkin.

His kitchen, despite its unused state, has napkins — linen, monogrammed, absurd. I borrow a pen from my bag and write carefully, the way Margery taught me to stitch: even, deliberate, each letter a small act of devotion.
*Thank you for the pasta. It was terrible. I'll teach you properly next time.*

Next time. The cruelest word I know. The most tender. A promise and a farewell pressed into the same four syllables. I have written variations of this note across centuries — on scraps of vellum, on the backs of playbills, on hotel stationery in cities that have since changed their names. The words change. The architecture doesn't.
I leave the napkin on the counter, beside the burned pot.

I stand. My legs are stiff, the cold of the floor having seeped through my tights hours ago and settled into my joints like a guest who doesn't know when to leave. I pick up my bag. I move toward the door.

The penthouse door is heavy — solid, soundproofed, designed to keep the world out. I pull it open as quietly as I can, the hinges silent, the door yielding like it owes me something. At the threshold, I turn.
Rowan hasn't moved.

He is still beside the window, head tipped back, one hand on his chest, the emerald catching the early light. The city is getting lighter behind him — the first gray of dawn seeping into the sky like ink into water. His face is soft in it, unguarded, and the dawn touches him the way I cannot: gently, without history, without the weight of having loved him before in rooms that no longer exist.
I stand at the threshold and I look at him and my throat is full of centuries and the hum is so quiet now, so terribly quiet, and I whisper:
"I have loved you in velvet, and I have loved you in silk."
The words are barely audible. A breath wearing the shape of a sentence. He doesn't stir. I didn't expect him to. I didn't say it for him to hear.
I close the door behind me.

The elevator descends. I watch the numbers tick down — 42, 41, 40 — and feel the hum in my bones, fading. I left. I always leave. That's the pattern, the rhythm, the choreography of a life that doesn't allow for staying. Every century: the meeting, the tenderness, the slow accumulating of truth, and then the leaving. Always the leaving.
And every time I do, the hum gets quieter.

Not because the danger is passing. Not because whatever this force is between us is resolving into something safe. The hum gets quieter because the grief of leaving is an emotional rupture — and rupture is the kind of thing that triggers a shift in the first place. The last time the hum went this quiet, I woke up in a different body in a different decade with no memory of how I got there, only the ache of a door I had just closed still echoing in my chest.
The elevator reaches the lobby. The doors open. I step out into the doorman's polite nod and the city's early gray and the hum is barely there now.
A whisper.
A thread.
A warning.
